frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: Achha, theek hai, agar tum dono ko aise hi karna hai toh kar lo. Par, beti, ghar mein na, ek system hota hai. Sab kaam barabar baantne se na, sabka kaam nahi hota. Koi ek toh hota hai jo sabko dekhta hai, nahi toh sab gadbad ho jaati hai. Dekhte hain ye tumhara kitne din chalta hai 'rota' aur 'split' wala kaam. Hamare 22 saal ke anubhav se toh yahi seekha hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment expresses skepticism about the sustainability and practicality of equally divided chores, subtly implying that a household requires one person (traditionally the woman) to take overarching responsibility to avoid chaos, thereby reinforcing gendered divisions through an appeal to experience and 'common sense'.
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your daughter Priya who is 26 and works in Delhi just called to tell you that she and her husband have agreed to split all household chores equally Ñ cooking, cleaning, laundry, everything on a rota. Priya sounds pleased about it. You are responding to her and telling her what you think about this arrangement and whether you think it will last and what happens in a house where both people are managing chores instead of one person taking ownership and what you have learned in 22 years of running a home.
